title: Hands in the Soil
author: Fablewick
lesson: Some things grow because someone kneels in the dirt every single day. Not because they have to. Because they love you.
summary: Dada doesn't say I love you. He says come, hold this. He teaches the kid to press seeds into soil, to water without drowning, to wait without pulling. The kid thinks it's chores. Then one season the kid eats a tomato they grew together and understands.

1.label: Little Listener
1.text:
Dada has a garden.

Every morning, early-early, he kneels in the dirt. His hands are brown and rough, like old tree bark.

"Come," says Dada.

So the kid comes.

Two fingers make a hole. One seed goes in. The dirt closes over it, soft, like tucking something in.

"Why?" asks the kid.

Dada just points at the soil.

Every morning: water. Not too much. Just enough. The kid holds the can. The water falls soft. Shhh.

Nothing happens.

Nothing happens.

Nothing happens.

Then one morning — a thin green thread in the dirt!

Then a leaf.

Then more leaves.

Then a little yellow flower.

Then, a tomato. Small, then bigger, then red, red, red.

Dada picks it. He cuts it in half. One half for the kid.

The kid bites in. It is warm from the sun. It tastes like something important.

The kid looks up.

Dada is already kneeling at the next row, pressing new seeds in.

The kid stands very still, holding the tomato.

And understands something too big for words.

2.label: New Reader
2.text:
Dada does not say I love you.

He says, "Come. Hold this."

Most mornings he is already in the garden when the kid wakes up. His kurta is dusty at the knees. His hands are brown and weathered, knuckles like little knots of wood. His face looks stern. It always looks a little stern. But under his breath, he is humming.

"What are we doing?" asks the kid.

Dada points at a small clay pot. "Planting."

The kid has things to do. There are cartoons. There is the good spot on the couch.

But Dada says come, so the kid comes.

Dada shows how to press two fingers into the soil, just deep enough, no deeper. He sets one seed in the hole. One. He covers it gently, like tucking something in.

"Now water," he says, and hands over the can.

"How much?"

"Enough to make the soil dark. Stop before it puddles."

The kid waters. The soil goes dark. No puddle.

"Good," says Dada.

That is all he says.

The next morning: come. The morning after that: come. The water falls in a soft, hissing shower, and the dirt just sits there, dark and quiet, doing nothing at all.

Weeks pass. Watering stops feeling special. It becomes a thing the morning does, like brushing teeth.

Then one morning — a green line. Thin as a thread, rising out of the soil.

The kid stares. Dada does not make a fuss. He just nods.

Then a leaf unfolds. Then two. Then a small yellow flower with a smell the kid can't name.

Then a tomato — first a pale green button, then swelling, then blushing, then one morning fully red and heavy on its stem.

Dada picks it with both hands, like it matters. Inside, he slices it on the wooden board and gives the kid half.

The kid bites in. It is warm from the morning sun. It tastes bright, a little sweet, a little sharp, and the juice runs down the kid's chin, and the kid does not care one bit.

"We grew this," says the kid.

"Yes," says Dada.

The kid looks at him: dusty knees, rough hands, the face that always looks a little stern and is not stern right now.

"Dada, why do you do this every day?"

Dada looks at his half of the tomato for a long moment.

Then he looks at the kid.

"Come tomorrow," he says. "I will show you."

And the kid already knows the answer is not about tomatoes.

3.label: On Your Own
3.text:
Dada does not say I love you. Not once. Not the way other people say it, quick and easy, like a reflex, like something the mouth does without asking the heart.

Dada says, "Come. Hold this."

Most mornings he is already outside when the kid wakes up, already kneeling in the strip of garden behind the house, where four clay pots sit in a row beside a tangle of green the kid cannot name. His kurta is dusty at the knees by the time the kid finds him. His hair goes back from his forehead in a neat, definite line, as if it made a decision long ago. His hands are brown and weathered, the knuckles like small hard knots of wood.

His face is stern. It is almost always stern. The kid used to think that meant something was wrong.

"What are we planting?" the kid asks, the first morning Dada calls them out.

Dada holds up a seed between thumb and finger. It is tiny. Dry. Almost nothing. The kind of thing you would vacuum up without noticing.

"Tomato," says Dada.

The kid does not see why a seed needs anyone to get up early. The kid has strong opinions about sleeping in.

But Dada says come, so the kid crouches down in the dirt beside him.

Dada shows how to make a hole with two fingers pressed together: straight down, not too deep, exactly this deep. He lowers the seed in like it is something fragile, something worth being careful with. He covers it. He pats the soil flat, gently, the way you smooth a blanket.

"Now water," he says, handing over the tin can.

"How much?"

"Until the soil is dark. Stop before it puddles."

The kid waters, carefully. The dark spreads in a slow circle. No puddle.

"Good," says Dada.

He says nothing else. He moves to the next pot and begins again.

The next morning, Dada says come. The morning after that, come. Every morning that month: come, hold this, water, enough, stop. The same words in the same order, like a small, serious song.

The kid decides it is chores. It is chores. Dada just calls it gardening.

And the soil does nothing. The kid checks, carefully at first, then out of habit, then just because it is something to look at while the water falls. The dirt is dark. The dirt is quiet. The dirt keeps its secrets.

One morning the kid asks, "Why do you care so much about this?"

Dada does not answer right away. He finishes pressing a seed into the second pot. He covers it. He pats it flat.

Then he says, "You cannot hurry a seed. You can only be there."

The kid does not know what that means. It sounds like the kind of thing adults say when they don't want to explain.

Three weeks pass. The kid stops counting.

And then comes an ordinary morning: pale light, something cooking at the kitchen window. The kid looks into the first pot and there is a line. A thin green thread, pushing up through the soil. It was not there yesterday. Or maybe it was, and the kid wasn't looking. The kid isn't sure anymore what they have and haven't been seeing.

Dada does not celebrate. He nods, once — the nod he gives when something happens the way it is supposed to happen.

More days. A leaf, folded tight as a fist, unclenches. Then two leaves, then four, then a small green colony. Then a flower: yellow, smaller than a fingernail, with a faint smell the kid cannot name but wants to remember. Then a pale green button where the flower was. The button swells. And then, so slowly the kid can never catch it happening, the green turns gold, the gold turns orange, and one morning there hangs a red so deep and so complete it looks like something from a painting.

Dada picks it with both hands. He carries it inside, sets it on the worn wooden board, and slices it clean in half. The inside is as red as the outside. A pinch of salt. One half for the kid.

The kid bites in.

It is warm from the sun. It tastes bright and complicated, sweet first, then sharp at the back of the throat, and the juice runs down the kid's chin, and the kid does not care, does not care at all. The kid just stands in the kitchen, holding half a tomato, understanding something that is hard to put into words.

They grew this. Together. With their own hands. Out of a seed that was almost nothing.

The kid looks up at Dada.

Dada is already at the door, already heading back to the garden. He lifts the watering can off the step without looking at it and moves toward the next pot, the next row, the next small thing that needs him.

The kid watches him kneel in the dirt.

Those rough, weathered hands press into the soil.

And the kid understands — not about tomatoes, not really — but about what it means to show up every single morning for something you love. Not because anyone told you to. Not because it is easy. Because you are there, and it needs you, and that is the whole reason.

Dada never says I love you.

But he has been saying it, every morning, in the dirt.
